_meta.description: State-level reference data: water law doctrine, supply/demand baselines, and demand sector composition. Used as fallback when live USGS/WRI data is unavailable.

water_law_types.prior_appropriation: First-in-time, first-in-right. Legal framework for water allocation creates organized rights holders who mobilize quickly when supply is threatened. Most western states.
water_law_types.riparian: Water rights attached to land adjacent to water body. Less organized politically; opposition tends to be more diffuse. Most eastern states.
water_law_types.hybrid: Mixed doctrine; typically riparian base with permit overlay.

_meta.supply_score_notes: 0=abundant, 100=critically stressed. Derived from WRI Aqueduct 4.0 state aggregates + USGS decennial water use reports.
_meta.demand_pressure_notes: 0=low demand relative to supply, 100=severe over-allocation. Combines total withdrawal rate vs. renewable supply.
_meta.ag_water_pct_notes: Approximate share of total state withdrawals used for irrigation/livestock. High values mean agricultural rights holders dominate water politics.
_meta.demand_trend_notes: Population + industrial growth pressure on water supply. 0=stable/declining, 100=rapid growth stress.

AZ.notes: CAP-dependent; groundwater overdraft in non-AMA counties; Colorado compact stress

NV.notes: Most arid; Las Vegas highly Lake Mead-dependent; limited recycled water uptake

NM.notes: Rio Grande over-allocated; Permian Basin drawdown; no large reservoir buffer

CA.notes: High spatial variation; Central Valley extreme; SGMA groundwater law in effect

UT.notes: Snowpack-dependent; Great Salt Lake decline signals basin stress; fast-growing urban demand

CO.notes: Headwaters state; compacted downstream; Front Range growth vs. fixed supply

WY.notes: Headwaters; low population; minimal industrial demand pressure

MT.notes: Largely low stress; snowpack-driven; east MT more variable

ID.notes: Snake River Plain GW-surface water connectivity; irrigation dominates

OR.notes: Willamette Valley low stress; eastern OR more arid; Klamath contested

WA.notes: Columbia/Snake high capacity; western WA very low; eastern WA irrigation demand

TX.notes: West TX extreme; Ogallala depletion in Panhandle; fast-growing metros

KS.notes: Ogallala depletion most severe nationally in western KS

OK.notes: Western OK Ogallala; 2011–12 drought shapes political memory

NE.notes: Ogallala GW drawdown in west; Platte relatively stable

SD.notes: Missouri River reservoirs provide buffer; western SD more stressed

ND.notes: Garrison Reservoir buffer; oil country water demand growing

MN.notes: Land of 10,000 Lakes; Great Lakes basin; strong groundwater recharge

WI.notes: Great Lakes access; high recharge; sand counties pumping emerging concern

MI.notes: Great Lakes jurisdiction; highest surface water availability

OH.notes: Lake Erie basin; abundant groundwater; industrial use historically high

IN.notes: Great Lakes/Ohio watershed; moderate stress; karst aquifer vulnerability

IL.notes: Chicago metro Great Lakes access; downstate surface water adequate

PA.notes: Abundant precipitation; Susquehanna/Delaware basins; low stress

NY.notes: Catskill/Delaware watersheds; Great Lakes western NY; excellent infrastructure

VA.notes: Northern VA growing demand vs. Potomac; Eastern Shore GW stress

GA.notes: Tri-state water wars (ACF basin); 2007–08 drought policy scar; Atlanta constrained

FL.notes: Floridan Aquifer over-pumped; saltwater intrusion coastal; wet/dry season stark split

SC.notes: Piedmont moderate; coastal low; Savannah River compact with GA

NC.notes: Generally adequate; Catawba-Wateree for Charlotte metro

TN.notes: TVA system large reservoir buffer; abundant precipitation

AL.notes: Generally low stress; ACF basin tensions; Mobile River ample

MS.notes: Mississippi Alluvial Plain GW declining from agriculture

AR.notes: Alluvial aquifer depletion from rice irrigation

MO.notes: Missouri/Mississippi systems; 2012 drought precedent; Ozark aquifer stable

KY.notes: Ohio/Kentucky River adequate; karst groundwater vulnerability

WV.notes: High precipitation; Ohio watershed; low demand pressure

MD.notes: Potomac-based; Chesapeake Bay watershed; Eastern Shore GW stress

DE.notes: Delaware River access; coastal plain aquifer heavy use

NJ.notes: High density; Passaic/Raritan/Delaware; Pinelands aquifer sensitive

CT.notes: Adequate surface water; moderate demand; CT River healthy

RI.notes: Narragansett watershed; adequate for current demand

MA.notes: Quabbin/Wachusett reservoirs; Cape Cod aquifer sensitive

VT.notes: High precipitation; Connecticut/Champlain basins; very low stress

NH.notes: Merrimack/Connecticut watersheds; low demand relative to supply

ME.notes: Lowest water stress in New England; abundant lakes and rivers

IA.notes: Des Moines/Iowa River; 2012 drought severe; nitrate contamination secondary concern

HI.notes: Extreme variation by island; leeward sides stressed; Maui aquifer contested

AK.notes: Glacially dominated; vast surface water; minimal industrial demand
